summary: Stancil argues that the classic innovator’s dilemma is breaking down because AI labs can ship generic models that accidentally cover niche products, blurring the line between platform and product. He extends this to cultural and corporate narratives: what once felt fundamental (launch events, polished messaging, quarterly reporting) can lose relevance as memes, AI, and narrative control reshape attention and disclosure. The result is a world where incumbents aren’t just slow to chase small markets; they may unintentionally subsume them while controlling the story around the data.
money_quotes: Now, when AI labs update their models or chat apps, products accidentally fall out.
If more and more software starts to look like a smart robot and a spreadsheet, the platform and the product are effectively the same thing.
Apple’s playbook is stale; its vocabulary is stale; its entire economic model of demand generation is stale.
That’s why AI could change how companies report data—not because it can write queries for Wall Street analysts, but because it can influence Wall Street analysts.
Everything becomes BI, but BI becomes the corporate spokesperson.
key_insight: As AI models grow more general, they collapse the distinction between platform and product, letting incumbents accidentally subsume niches while reshaping how culture and corporate narratives are controlled.
